evidence: Docstring 'Original Hall's conjecture with exponent 1/2' under the module header referencing Wikipedia's Hall's conjecture page.
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); Q1165804 label matches; decl states exponent-1/2 Hall conjecture exactly.

evidence: Docstring: 'Danilov proved that one cannot replace the exponent 1/2 with larger number' — a solved sharpness result about Hall's conjecture, not the conjecture itself.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); Danilov sharpness result is directly about Hall's conjecture exponent — load-bearing mention.

evidence: Docstring: 'Weak form of Hall's conjecture: relax the exponent from 1/2 to 1/2 - epsilon' — a variant, not the exact conjecture.
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); weak variant of Hall's conjecture, correctly kind=mentions not formalizes.

evidence: Module header '# First Hardy–Littlewood conjecture' with the Wikipedia reference; the decl states the prime-constellation asymptotic pi_P(n) ~ C_P integral.
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); QID denotes this very conjecture. Formal statement uses =O rather than ~, but it is the repo's declared formalization of the first HL conjecture.

evidence: Section '## Second Hardy-Littlewood Conjecture' with its Wikipedia URL comment; the decl states pi(x+y) <= pi(x) + pi(y) for x, y >= 2.
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); states pi(x+y) <= pi(x)+pi(y) for x,y>=2 — exactly Q2920423.

evidence: Docstring: 'where pi(z) denotes the prime-counting function, giving the number of primes up to and including z'.
verify_note: pi (prime-counting function) is the sole object in the statement — load-bearing.

evidence: Docstring: 'Richards [Ri74] showed that only one of the two Hardy-Littlewood conjectures can be true'.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); the incompatibility theorem takes the first conjecture as hypothesis — load-bearing.

evidence: Docstring: 'Richards [Ri74] showed that only one of the two Hardy-Littlewood conjectures can be true' — the incompatibility statement involves the second conjecture as hypothesis target.
verify_note: The negated conclusion is the second conjecture — load-bearing.

evidence: Module header 'Idoneal numbers completeness conjecture' asserts the list of 65 idoneal numbers is complete, referencing Wikipedia's Idoneal number page; no Wikidata item exists for the completeness conjecture itself.
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); idoneal number is the defining concept of the completeness conjecture; correctly mentions since no QID for the conjecture itself.

evidence: Docstring '**Inscribed square problem** Does every Jordan curve admit an inscribed square?' matches the module header's Wikipedia reference to the inscribed square problem (Toeplitz conjecture).
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); answer-form statement of the Toeplitz/inscribed-square problem itself — exact.

evidence: Module header: 'every Jordan curve (i.e. simple close curve in R²)' — the statement quantifies over embeddings of the circle, i.e. Jordan curves.
verify_note: Quantifies over embeddings Circle -> R^2, i.e. Jordan curves; Q1392659 alias 'Jordan curve' — load-bearing.

evidence: Docstring '**Inscribed rectangle problem** Does every Jordan curve admit inscribed rectangles of any given aspect ratio?' — an aspect-ratio variant of the inscribed square problem, not the square statement itself.
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); aspect-ratio rectangle variant, correctly mentions not formalizes.

evidence: The statement quantifies over Jordan curves (embeddings Circle -> R²), per the module header's 'every Jordan curve' phrasing.
verify_note: Statement quantifies over Jordan curves — load-bearing.

evidence: Docstring 'It is known that every Jordan curve admits at least one inscribed rectangle' — a solved variant listed under the inscribed square problem reference.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); solved variant filed under the inscribed square problem — appropriate mention.

evidence: Docstring 'every *smooth* Jordan curve admits inscribed rectangles of all aspect ratios' — a solved smooth-case variant of the inscribed square problem.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); smooth-case solved variant of the problem — appropriate mention.

evidence: Docstring 'every C² Jordan curve admits an inscribed square' — the solved C² case of the inscribed square problem, not the full open conjecture.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); C^2 solved case of the same problem — appropriate mention.

evidence: Module header '# Invariant Subspace Problem' with Wikipedia reference; the decl states every bounded operator on a separable complex Hilbert space of dim >= 2 has a non-trivial closed invariant subspace.
verify_note: Decl exists (research open); states the ISP for bounded operators on separable complex Hilbert spaces dim>=2 — exact.

evidence: Docstring: 'every bounded linear operator T : H -> H on a separable Hilbert space H of dimension at least 2'.
verify_note: Hilbert space is the ambient structure of the statement — load-bearing.

evidence: Solved finite-dimensional case: 'Every (bounded) linear operator T on a finite-dimensional linear space H of dimension at least 2 has a non-trivial (closed) T-invariant subspace'.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); finite-dimensional solved case of the ISP — appropriate mention.

evidence: Solved non-separable case: 'Every bounded linear operator T on a non-separable Hilbert space H has a non-trivial closed T-invariant subspace'.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved, with formal_proof); non-separable solved case — appropriate mention.

evidence: Solved normal-operator case: 'Every normal linear operator T on a Hilbert space H of dimension at least 2 has a non-trivial closed T-invariant subspace'.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); normal-operator solved case — appropriate mention.

evidence: Read/Enflo counterexample on l1: 'There exists a bounded linear operator T on the l1 space ... without non-trivial closed T-invariant subspace [Read 1985]'.
verify_note: Decl exists (research solved); Read/Enflo l1 counterexample is directly about the ISP — appropriate mention.
